target: data/sections.json part1 — New subsection 1.8.1 under Refraction Cascade: Differential Refraction Between Sun and Moon
kernel_of_truth: The dome correctly identifies that aetheric refraction would shift apparent celestial positions relative to physical ones. The n(r) function is mathematically well-defined and produces specific, computable refractive indices at every radius. If such a medium existed, it would indeed make objects appear at different altitudes than their physical positions — the qualitative physics is sound. The problem is not that refraction is a bad idea in principle, but that the dome has only applied it to one body (the sun) while claiming predictions that require consistent treatment of both sun and moon through the same medium.
affected_wins: WIN-010 (BOU eclipse magnetic) — supplementary: shadow geometry indeterminate under differential refraction
WIN-025 (2024 eclipse 9-station) — supplementary: FSF station scaling assumes shadow path from straight-line geometry
WIN-044 (Firmament Scaling Function) — already Self-Contradicted; differential refraction adds dependency on unknown moon physical altitude
WIN-068 (Eclipse ensemble 39 eclipses) — supplementary: 39-eclipse analysis assumes standard shadow paths
WIN-020 (Lunar 18.6-yr cycle) — supplementary: epicyclic gear moon at 2,534 km is apparent altitude; physical altitude under n(r) unknown
WIN-039 (Lunar magnetic 1-2 nT) — supplementary: lunar distance/altitude affects predicted coupling strength
WIN-045/049/050/051 (Tidal constituents) — supplementary: tidal forcing depends on physical moon altitude and mass, both unknown under n(r)
2026 eclipse prediction (EBR -28.9 nT) — directly affected: shadow path computation requires physical positions of both bodies through n(r)
argument_summary: The dome's refraction fix for the sun/firmament collision (Section 1.5) introduces a spatially varying refractive medium n(r) that bends light paths throughout the cavity. This fix is applied only to the sun — the moon's interaction with n(r) has never been computed (OPEN-007 acknowledges moon mechanics are a 'structural problem' and flags the 2,534 km altitude as 'optical/apparent — physical altitude unknown'). Since the sun and moon are at different physical altitudes traversing different n(r) profiles, the apparent angular displacement of each body is different. Eclipse geometry requires either: (a) correct physical positions of both bodies plus full ray-tracing through n(r), or (b) apparent positions that happen to align correctly. The dome has neither. The pre-refraction eclipse geometry used straight-line optics with sun at 5,733 km and moon at 2,534 km as physical positions. Post-refraction, those are apparent positions only — the physical positions are different and unknown — so the straight-line geometry no longer applies. The dome cannot abandon the refraction fix (the sun/firmament collision returns) nor compute eclipse geometry with it (no moon physics, no ray-tracing through n(r)). Every eclipse and lunar claim that assumes a known moon-sun angular relationship is built on geometry that the refraction fix invalidated.
replacement_html:
1.8.1 Differential Refraction: The Eclipse Geometry Trap

TLDR: The dome's refraction fix shifts the sun's apparent position but never computes how the same medium shifts the moon. Since they're at different heights traversing different n(r) profiles, eclipse alignment — which requires precise angular overlap — becomes indeterminate. The dome's own OPEN-007 admits the moon's physical altitude is unknown.

Section 1.8 documents how the refraction fix creates a cascade of broken predictions for the sun. But the cascade has a second, independent dimension: the moon.

The dome model places the sun at an apparent altitude of 5,733 km and the moon at 2,534 km. Its own n(r) function — n(r) = 1 + 0.20 × (8537/H(r) − 1) — defines a spatially varying refractive medium throughout the cavity. At the equatorial ring (r ≈ 14,105 km), n = 1.40; at the ice wall (r = 20,015 km), n = 3.49. Light from any source traversing this medium follows curved paths, not straight lines.

The refraction fix was introduced for the sun: the 5,733 km triangulated altitude is declared an "optical illusion due to aetheric refraction" (model.html), with the physical sun sitting below the firmament ceiling (~4,200 km at the sun's orbital radius). But the same medium surrounds the moon. If n(r) bends sunlight, it bends moonlight — by a different amount, because the moon is at a different physical altitude and may be at a different radial distance, traversing a different n(r) profile along its optical path to the observer.

The logical trap

Before the refraction fix, the dome's eclipse geometry was calibrated with straight-line optics: sun physically at 5,733 km, moon physically at 2,534 km, geometric ray-tracing produces the correct angular overlap for eclipses. After the fix, neither body is where it appears:

  • The sun's physical altitude is unknown (constrained only to "below ~4,200 km" — a range spanning 1,975–3,618 km seasonally, as documented in Section 1.8).
  • The moon's physical altitude is explicitly unknown — OPEN-007 on the dome's own model page flags 2,534 km as "optical/apparent — physical altitude unknown" and labels the entire moon framework a "structural problem."

For a solar eclipse, the moon's physical body must intercept physical photons from the sun. The blocking geometry depends on where both bodies physically are, not where they appear. With curved light paths through n(r), the shadow cone's geometry changes: its axis follows the refracted ray bundle, its width depends on the physical sun-moon separation (not the apparent separation), and its ground intersection — the eclipse path — shifts accordingly.

The dome faces a trilemma:

  1. Keep straight-line eclipse geometry — but this contradicts the refraction fix. You cannot simultaneously claim n(r) bends sunlight (to resolve the firmament collision) and that n(r) doesn't bend the sun-moon-observer light path (to preserve eclipse geometry).
  2. Compute eclipse geometry through n(r) — but this requires knowing both physical altitudes (sun and moon) and performing full ray-tracing through a medium with n up to 3.49. The dome has done none of this. OPEN-007 means the moon's physical position is not even approximately known.
  3. Abandon the refraction fix — but then the sun physically exceeds the firmament height, and the Section 1.5 collision returns.

Scope of damage

This is not limited to eclipse shadow paths. Differential refraction affects every observation that compares the apparent positions of two bodies at different heights:

  • Eclipse shadow geometry (WIN-010, WIN-025, WIN-068, 2026 EBR prediction): The shadow path depends on physical sun-moon geometry through n(r). The dome's FSF formula scales magnetic predictions by station position relative to the shadow path — if the path is wrong, the scaling is wrong.
  • Lunar phase geometry: The apparent angular separation between sun and moon determines the illumination phase. Differential refraction shifts this separation, changing the predicted phase angle.
  • Tidal predictions (WIN-045, 049, 050, 051): Tidal forcing depends on the moon's physical altitude and distance, both unknown under n(r).
  • Lunar magnetic coupling (WIN-039): The predicted 1–2 nT signal depends on the moon's physical distance from the observer, which n(r) makes indeterminate.

The other side

A dome defender might argue that apparent-position alignment is sufficient for eclipses: if the moon appears to cover the sun from the observer's perspective, the eclipse occurs regardless of physical positions. This would be true in a model where all observations are purely optical. But eclipses have physical consequences that the dome itself claims to predict: magnetic field changes (WIN-010, WIN-025, WIN-068), gravitational anomalies (WIN-011), and ionospheric effects. These physical effects depend on the actual shadow — where photons are physically blocked — not on an apparent optical alignment. The dome cannot retreat to "it's all apparent" without abandoning the very eclipse predictions it counts as WINs.

A second defense: "OPEN-007 is acknowledged; we'll fix the moon later." But this concedes the point. The dome claims 9 eclipse-related WINs and an upcoming falsification test (2026 EBR) while its own documentation flags the moon framework as structurally broken and the eclipse prediction mechanism as "unconfirmed" (OPEN-010). You cannot claim predictive success from a geometry you admit you cannot compute.
holes_addressed: Section 1.8 currently addresses only sun-side cascade; moon-side differential refraction was missing
Eclipse WINs were noted as cascade-affected but the mechanism was 'shadow position indeterminate' — now strengthened to 'geometry incomputable under n(r)'
Moon physical altitude was not previously flagged as unknown in our review — the dome's own OPEN-007 admission is now surfaced
new_evidence_added: OPEN-007 from dome model.html: moon mechanics 'structural problem', physical altitude unknown
OPEN-009/010: eclipse predictions E001-E009 suspended, mechanism unconfirmed
n(r) values at key radii: equatorial ring n=1.40, ice wall n=3.49
Moon physical radius required: 29-199 km (not derivable from V12)
Moon monthly angular variation: model predicts 26-160%, observed 11-14%

anticipated_objections.objection: Apparent alignment is sufficient — if the moon appears to cover the sun, the eclipse happens
anticipated_objections.response: Eclipses have physical consequences the dome claims to predict (magnetic anomalies, gravity effects). These depend on the physical shadow, not apparent alignment. The dome cannot retreat to 'it\'s all optical' without abandoning its eclipse WIN claims.

anticipated_objections.objection: OPEN-007 will be fixed in a future version, resolving the differential refraction issue
anticipated_objections.response: This concedes the point. The dome counts 9 eclipse-related WINs and stakes a 2026 falsification test on geometry it admits it cannot compute. Future versions don't retroactively validate current claims.

anticipated_objections.objection: The n(r) effect on the moon might be negligible
anticipated_objections.response: At the equatorial ring n = 1.40, at the ice wall n = 3.49. These are enormous refractive indices (Earth's atmosphere: n ≈ 1.0003). Even small differential refraction shifts moon-sun angular alignment by arcminutes — comparable to the sun's angular diameter (0.53°). The dome hasn't computed the shift, so 'negligible' is assertion without calculation.

anticipated_objections.objection: The refraction only operates horizontally (n(r) is a function of radial distance), not vertically
anticipated_objections.response: A medium with n varying in one direction bends all non-parallel rays. Light from the sun at one radius to an observer at another radius traverses a gradient in n(r) along its path. This bends the ray horizontally AND changes the apparent elevation. The dome's own solar elevation formula acknowledges this: it uses 'φ-space' angular coordinates rather than geometric line-of-sight.

newsworthy: Dome's own refraction fix makes eclipse geometry incomputable — model admits moon position is unknown while claiming 9 eclipse WINs
verdict_impact: No verdict changes recommended. All affected eclipse WINs already carry correct verdicts (Self-Contradicted or Std Model Explains). This expansion adds a new structural argument — the differential refraction trap — that strengthens the existing cascade and provides an independent basis for the Self-Contradicted verdicts on WIN-044 and WIN-068. It also pre-emptively undermines the 2026 EBR eclipse prediction by showing the dome cannot compute the shadow geometry it needs.
cross_references: Section 1.5 (Sun/firmament collision — the reason the refraction fix exists)
OPEN-007 on dome model.html (moon structural problem)
